source: Finding Users Who Act Alike- Transfer Learning for Expanding Advertiser Audiences.pdf

concepts.finding:
1.2
Unique Contributions to Look-alike
Advertising
Our approach introduces the following novel contributions to the
field of look-alike advertising:
• Modeling users in an embedding space, yielding user embed-
dings and a user-user similarity metric.
concepts.implication: Useful for building item/user embeddings in retrieval systems

concepts.finding:
In Sections 3.2 and 3.3, we present
a novel model which combines a learned user embedding and ap-
proximate retrieval techniques and is the main focus of this work.
concepts.implication: Useful for building item/user embeddings in retrieval systems

concepts.finding:
Although the model is trained to find
similarity between a user and a piece of content, it will be applied to
the transfer problem of user-to-advertiser similarity.
concepts.implication: Relevant for similarity-based retrieval and lookalike targeting

concepts.finding:
Finally, we propose an
ensemble approach that combines single-advertiser classifiers with
the embedding-based technique.
concepts.implication: Useful for building item/user embeddings in retrieval systems

concepts.finding:
This includes the ability to show
ads to specific demographic groups, people who are searching for
specific keywords, or users who have expressed prior interest in
specific topics.
concepts.implication: Can inform intent modeling for recommendation systems

concepts.finding:
We will demonstrate in
experiments in section 5 that this method outperforms a classifier-
based baseline for small seeds, and that an ensemble of embedding-
and classifier-based models performs best for all seed list sizes.
concepts.implication: Useful for building item/user embeddings in retrieval systems

concepts.finding:
The embedding model significantly improves on the
classifier baseline for both precision and recall, and the blended
model improves these metrics even further.
concepts.implication: Useful for building item/user embeddings in retrieval systems

concepts.finding:
We evaluated all three models’
performance on CPC (Cost-per-click) ads, an important format for
which the advertiser’s goal is user clicks and our goal is to achieve a
high click rate.
concepts.implication: Directly applicable to CTR/CVR prediction in advertising systems

concepts.finding:
Overall, the embedding-only model de-
creased CTR compared to the classifier model, but the blended
model improved CTR.
concepts.implication: Useful for building item/user embeddings in retrieval systems

concepts.finding:
Finally,
we described how to blend data from classifier- and embedding-
based approaches to improve offline and online performance for all
seed list sizes.
concepts.implication: Useful for building item/user embeddings in retrieval systems

concepts.finding:
For
the first stage we trained a global user embedding model on sitewide
user activity logs.
concepts.implication: Useful for building item/user embeddings in retrieval systems

concepts.finding:
In the second stage, we use transfer learning and
statistical techniques to create lightweight seed list representations
in the embedding space for each advertiser.

concepts.implication: Useful for building item/user embeddings in retrieval systems

concepts.finding:
We should be able to mea-
sure how well users are matched to advertisers by measuring
overall user engagement metrics such as number of clicks,
number of ads viewed, etc.
concepts.implication: Directly applicable to CTR/CVR prediction in advertising systems

concepts.finding:
More specifically, we break the modeling problem into two parts: (a)
building a universal model of user-user similarity based on organic
data, to be shared between all advertisers, and (b) representing
each advertiser’s seed list of users in a way that builds from our
understanding of user-user simi
concepts.implication: Relevant for similarity-based retrieval and lookalike targeting

concepts.finding:
By sharing the universal user-similarity model
amongst all advertisers, the second task of building a light-weight
model of advertiser seed lists can be done using computationally
cheap methods that provide meaningful results even for small seed
lists.
concepts.implication: Relevant for similarity-based retrieval and lookalike targeting

concepts.finding:
The literature around word-embeddings (word2vec [8], GloVe
[9]) has shown that embeddings can be trained so that dot-products
and distances in a low-dimensional embedding space capture simi-
larity and relationships.
concepts.implication: Useful for building item/user embeddings in retrieval systems

concepts.finding:
We introduce advertiser information by building a lightweight
representation that summarizes the advertiser’s seed list users.
concepts.implication: Useful for building item/user embeddings in retrieval systems
